CLARITY.justification: The excerpt shows a recognizable high-level structure (methods, datasets, experiments) but is poorly organized and edited: many sentences and paragraphs are duplicated (e.g. the Dridan (2013) and Flickinger/Packard bridging passages recur verbatim), section fragments are interleaved and sometimes truncated, and dataset/table text is garbled (the Dataset Items/#words table line is unreadable). Notation and key terms like MRS and EDM are introduced, but presentation is inconsistent and scattered, and stray artifacts ("LaTeX with hyperref package") and mixed fragments impede clarity. Overall the writing needs substantial cleanup and reorganization to present a clear logical flow and readable figures/tables.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper studies robust parsing methods for HPSG using well-known resources (the ERG, Redwoods treebank) and realistic datasets (ws13r, semcor, wsj00ab) and evaluation metric (EDM), which squarely fits the interests of the NLP community. It includes comparisons of multiple techniques (bridging, PCFG approximation/csaw, hybrid approaches), uses statistical ranking and evaluation practices expected at top NLP venues, and cites/positions itself within the HPSG and parsing literature (Flickinger, Oepen, Zhang et al.), making it appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the concrete and important problem of robustness/coverage for precision HPSG grammars (ERG), which directly affects real-world applications (e.g. customer email response, information extraction) and treebank maintenance. It systematically compares multiple practical techniques (bridging, csaw/PCFG approximation, hybrid methods and resource-baseline) and evaluation choices (EDM, curated out-of-coverage datasets), reporting large empirical gains (coverage gaps reduced by 75–100% while improving EDM F1). That empirical, evaluation-focused contribution is likely to change practitioner choices and spawn follow-up work (e.g. better disambiguation for bridging, hybrid integration), making it of strong impact within grammar engineering even if its influence beyond the HPSG/precision-grammar community is more limited.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates multiple robust parsing methods across several curated datasets (alchemy45, ws13r, semcor, wsj00ab) and uses an MRS-based metric (EDM) with concrete mitigation for grammar-version mismatches, and reports coverage and EDM F1 changes (e.g. "coverage gaps can be reduced by between 75% and 100%''). Related work is extensively cited (Fouvry, Dridan, Flickinger, Zhang & Krieger, Oepen et al.) and several failure modes are discussed (ACE unpacking failures, bridging ambiguity, rare cyclic robust-unification failure). However, ablation/sensitivity analyses are not presented for key design choices (e.g. no disambiguation training for bridging, thresholds for supertagging), and some reproducibility-relevant details are missing or deferred (EDM metric still "In prep", limited implementation/configuration details), so the study is strong but has minor gaps.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly describes methods (baseline ERG with extended resources, bridging, csaw/PCFG approximation, hybrid techniques) and datasets (alchemy45, ws13r, semcor, wsj00ab) and takes plausible steps to mitigate evaluation issues across grammar versions (EDM partial credit, ignoring certain predicate fields). However, the provided text contains no quantitative results, tables, or statistical tests to substantiate the headline claim that coverage gaps are reduced “between 75% and 100%,” nor does it report significance testing or detailed experimental controls. The use of a fabricated “alchemy” gold set and the acknowledged grammar-version mismatch introduce potential bias that is discussed but not empirically characterized, so methodological and evidentiary support is incomplete though the experimental design elements are reasonable.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper primarily evaluates and recombines existing techniques rather than proposing a wholly new method: it reimplements and compares bridging (Flickinger & Packard 2015), a csaw/PCFG approximation combining JigSaw (Zhang & Krieger 2011) and Zhang et al.’s robust unification, and cites prior work such as Dridan (2013) and Fouvry (2003). It does contain modest, non-trivial extensions and clear differentiation from prior work — e.g. the hybrid-tb/hybrid-ww approach that mixes csaw constituents into chart parsing (Sec. 3.4.5), and adapted evaluation choices (EDM partial credit, alchemy/ws13r dataset construction). Overall this is an incremental but useful comparative study that would inform practitioners, though it is not highly original.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates multiple appropriate baselines from the HPSG literature (ERG baseline with raised resources; bridging (Flickinger & Packard 2015); csaw / PCFG approximations (Zhang & Krieger 2011 and Zhang et al.); hybrids; and prior work such as Dridan 2013, Fouvry 2003, Adolphs et al. 2008 and Bender et al. 2004) on shared datasets (alchemy45, ws13r, semcor, wsj00ab) using EDM and with explicit mitigations for grammar-version mismatches. Comparisons are mostly fair and clearly described (same data/metrics, explicit baseline), and the paper acknowledges when prior methods succeed or have limits (e.g. Dridan’s gains, bridging’s unpacking failures) and analyzes methodological differences. Minor concerns are that some gold data come from different grammar versions (necessitating mitigations) and the primary evaluation metric is cited as “Zhang et al. (In prep)”, which slightly weakens reproducibility, hence not a perfect (5) rating.
